food_subgroup: Rice paper rolls / fresh spring rolls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 covers spring roll as a general category; no specific vegetarian fresh (non-fried) rice-paper roll entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers spring rolls generically; no vegetarian fresh rice-paper variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for vegetarian fresh spring rolls. No code assigned.

allergens: soy (vegetarian cold cuts may contain soy)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 pieces of vegetarian fresh spring rolls, approximately 200 g total, as typically served at a Vietnamese-Singaporean restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Popiah (Fresh Spring Roll) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for vegetarian fresh rice-paper rolls
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fresh popiah (fresh spring roll) as the closest Singaporean analogue. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo fresh popiah entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for fresh Vietnamese-style spring rolls (rice paper, vegetables, herbs). The dish is vegetarian and unfried; values reflect the fresh, unfried, vegetable-dominant composition. Per-100g values are estimated from the composite of rice paper (~35%), vegetables and herbs (~45%), rice vermicelli (~15%), and vegetarian cold cuts (~5%) by weight.

gi_source.title: Rice paper rolls (fresh spring rolls, Vietnamese-style) — analogous estimate based on rice paper and vegetable composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for vegetarian fresh spring rolls (Gỏi Cuốn Chay). GI estimated at 40 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice paper (GI ~40–50, low category) and the dominant vegetable and herb content which further lowers the overall GI. The dish is unfried, vegetable-dominant, and wrapped in thin rice paper; the composite GI is conservatively estimated at 40 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 39.0) / 100 = 15.6, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 39.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from vegetarian cold cuts and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 360 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation, as vegetarian cold cuts and dipping sauces can contribute additional sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of ~15.6 per 200 g serving, vegetarian fresh spring rolls are a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces; the high vegetable and herb content supports glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, no cholesterol, high vegetable content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetarian fresh spring rolls are low in saturated fat (~0.3 g/100 g) and contain no cholesterol, making them a heart-friendly option; however, sodium from cold cuts and dipping sauces should be limited for individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Fresh Spring Rolls (Gỏi Cuốn Chay) are a Vietnamese-origin dish widely served in Singapore, consisting of thin, translucent rice paper wrappers filled with vegetarian cold cuts, fresh vegetables such as lettuce, cucumber, and carrot, rice vermicelli noodles, and aromatic Vietnamese herbs including mint, perilla, and coriander. The rolls are assembled fresh without frying, giving them a light, delicate texture and a clean, herbaceous flavour profile. They are served cold or at room temperature, typically accompanied by a hoisin-peanut or sweet chilli dipping sauce. Nutritionally, they are low in fat and calories (~105 kcal/100 g), with a moderate carbohydrate content derived primarily from rice paper and vermicelli, and a low glycemic index (~40), making them suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The vegetable and herb content provides dietary fibre, vitamin A, and vitamin C. The dish is fully vegetarian and dairy-free. Sodium content is moderate and largely attributable to the vegetarian cold cuts and any accompanying dipping sauce. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Vietnamese restaurants and is popular as a light starter or snack. The dish is also known as Goi Cuon Chay in romanized Vietnamese.
common_user_queries: Calories in vegetarian fresh spring rolls
Is Goi Cuon suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for vegetarian fresh spring rolls (Gỏi Cuốn Chay). Values are adapted from the closest HPB Foodo fresh popiah entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for fresh Vietnamese-style rice paper rolls. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on rice paper GI values. Confidence is medium due to ingredient-level estimation rather than a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fresh, unfried vegetarian dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per composition analysis. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients (vegetarian cold cuts are plant-based). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products providing B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this dish contains no animal-derived or fortified ingredients providing vitamin D. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from HPB Foodo fresh popiah and USDA FoodData Central vegetable/herb/rice paper composite; these are not -1 as reasonable estimates are available. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database rice paper analogue; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. Serving size of 200 g is based on the restaurant description of 4 pieces per serving, with each piece estimated at approximately 50 g. Price data (SGD 21.88) is preserved from the field survey source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. Vegan flag is set to false as some vegetarian cold cuts may contain egg derivatives; this should be verified with the specific restaurant.
